The **Princely Abbey of Stavelot-Malmedy**, also **Principality of Stavelot-Malmedy**, sometimes known with its German name **Stablo**, was an [ecclesiastical principality](/wiki/Hochstift "Hochstift") of the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire"). Princely power was exercised by the [Benedictine](/wiki/Order_of_Saint_Benedict "Order of Saint Benedict") abbot of the imperial double monastery of [Stavelot](/wiki/Stavelot "Stavelot") and [Malmedy](/wiki/Malmedy "Malmedy"), founded in 651. Along with the [Duchy of *Bouillon*](/wiki/Duchy_of_Bouillon "Duchy of Bouillon") and the [Prince-Bishopric of *Liège*](/wiki/Prince-Bishopric_of_Li%C3%A8ge "Prince-Bishopric of Liège"), it was one of only three principalities of the Southern Netherlands that were never part of the [Spanish Netherlands](/wiki/Spanish_Netherlands "Spanish Netherlands"), later the [Austrian Netherlands](/wiki/Austrian_Netherlands "Austrian Netherlands"),[[1]](#cite_note-Uyttebrouck-1) which after 1500 were assigned to the [Burgundian Circle](/wiki/Burgundian_Circle "Burgundian Circle") while the principalities were assigned to the [Lower Rhenish](/wiki/Lower_Rhenish%E2%80%93Westphalian_Circle "Lower Rhenish–Westphalian Circle") [Imperial Circle](/wiki/Imperial_Circle "Imperial Circle").[[2]](#cite_note-wikisource:Imperial_Circles-2)

As a prince-abbot, the abbot of Stavelot-Malmedy sat on the Ecclesiastical Bench of the College of Ruling Princes of the [Imperial Diet](/wiki/Imperial_Diet_(Holy_Roman_Empire) "Imperial Diet (Holy Roman Empire)") alongside the prince-bishops. Along with the handful of other prince-abbots, he cast a full vote (**votum virile**),[[3]](#cite_note-3) in contrast to the majority of imperial abbots who were only entitled to collectively determine the votes of their respective curial benches.

In 1795, the principality was abolished and its territory was incorporated into the [French *département*](/wiki/Departments_of_France#Departments_of_the_Napoleonic_Empire_in_Europe "Departments of France") of *[Ourthe](/wiki/Ourthe_(department) "Ourthe (department)")*.[[4]](#cite_note-Guide_pittoresque-4) The [Congress of Vienna](/wiki/Congress_of_Vienna "Congress of Vienna") in 1815 assigned Stavelot to the [United Kingdom of the Netherlands](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_the_Netherlands "United Kingdom of the Netherlands"),[[5]](#cite_note-Stavelot:Histoire-5) and Malmedy became part of the [Prussian](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Prussia "Kingdom of Prussia") [district](/wiki/Districts_of_Prussia "Districts of Prussia") of *[Eupen-Malmedy](/wiki/Eupen-Malmedy "Eupen-Malmedy")*.[[5]](#cite_note-Stavelot:Histoire-5) Both are currently parts of the [Kingdom of Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium")—since the 1830 [Belgian Revolution](/wiki/Belgian_Revolution "Belgian Revolution") and the 1919 [Treaty of Versailles](/wiki/Treaty_of_Versailles "Treaty of Versailles"), respectively (Malmedy annexed to Belgium in 1925). In 1921 the Abbey church of Malmedy became the Cathedral of the short-lived [Diocese of Eupen-Malmedy](/wiki/Diocese_of_Eupen-Malmedy "Diocese of Eupen-Malmedy").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Princely_Abbey_of_Stavelot-Malmedy&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Establishment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Princely_Abbey_of_Stavelot-Malmedy&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Establishment")]

[![A painted statue of a man in roughly his 30s, wearing a golden mitre and priestly robes in red, blue and gold. He holds a Bible in his left hand and an animal, presumably a wolf, standing at his feet.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/Remaclus_in_de_Sint-Sebastiaanskerk_van_Stavelot.jpg/120px-Remaclus_in_de_Sint-Sebastiaanskerk_van_Stavelot.jpg)](/wiki/File:Remaclus_in_de_Sint-Sebastiaanskerk_van_Stavelot.jpg)

[Saint Remaclus](/wiki/Remaclus "Remaclus")

[Saint Remaclus](/wiki/Remaclus "Remaclus") founded the Abbey of Stavelot on the *[Amblève](/wiki/Ambl%C3%A8ve_(river) "Amblève (river)")* river, circa 650,[[5]](#cite_note-Stavelot:Histoire-5)[[6]](#cite_note-em:Stavelot-6) on lands along the border between the bishoprics of [Cologne](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_Cologne "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Cologne") and *[Tongeren](/wiki/Bishopric_of_Tongeren "Bishopric of Tongeren")*,[[7]](#cite_note-fotw:Malmedy-7) this territory belonged at that time to Grimoald, the Austrasian mayor of the palace and member of the Arnulfinger-Peppinid family. A charter of [Sigebert III](/wiki/Sigebert_III "Sigebert III"), king of [Austrasia](/wiki/Austrasia "Austrasia") entrusted Remaclus with the monasteries of both Stavelot and Malmedy, which was located a few kilometres eastwards in the *[Ardennes](/wiki/Ardennes "Ardennes")* forest, "a place of horror and solitary isolation which abounds with wild beasts".[[8]](#cite_note-Carolingians_pp21-2-8)[[9]](#cite_note-Liège:Stavelot-9)[[10]](#cite_note-Délices-10) Sigebert granted forest land; charged his [Mayor of the Palace](/wiki/Mayor_of_the_Palace "Mayor of the Palace"), [Grimoald the Elder](/wiki/Grimoald_the_Elder "Grimoald the Elder"), with furnishing money to build the two monasteries; and continued to foster these communities with personal gifts.[[8]](#cite_note-Carolingians_pp21-2-8)

The site of Malmedy was probably already settled before the foundation of the abbey, despite [etymology](/wiki/Etymology "Etymology") seeming to indicate Malmedy's unsuitability.[[7]](#cite_note-fotw:Malmedy-7) **Mal(u)mund(a)-arium** was "a place with winding waters", or, most probably, **Malmund-arium**, a "bad confluency".[[7]](#cite_note-fotw:Malmedy-7) The *[Warchenne](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warchenne "fr:Warchenne")* was partially [canalised](/wiki/Canal "Canal") and its banks strengthened, to prevent the flooding that Malmedy often experienced.[[7]](#cite_note-fotw:Malmedy-7) The abbey church in Malmedy was dedicated to [St Benedict](/wiki/Benedict_of_Nursia "Benedict of Nursia").[[10]](#cite_note-Délices-10)
The monastery of Malmedy is considered by historians and hagiographers to be slightly older than the monastery of Stavelot,[[11]](#cite_note-1911:Malmedy-11) with the town claiming its foundation date as 648.[[12]](#cite_note-Malmedy:Growth-12) Malmedy is listed on earlier maps than Stavelot, and the commission appointed in 670 by [Childeric II](/wiki/Childeric_II "Childeric II"), in order to delimit the abbey territory, started from Malmedy ([Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language"): *de Monasterio Malmunderio*).[[7]](#cite_note-fotw:Malmedy-7) Afterwards, the territory of the abbey was enlarged westwards, so that Stavelot became the geographical centre and the capital of the principality.[[7]](#cite_note-fotw:Malmedy-7)

The first church in Stavelot was built by abbot [Godwin](/wiki/Godwin_of_Stavelot "Godwin of Stavelot") and, on 25 June 685, was dedicated to saints [Martin](/wiki/Martin_of_Tours "Martin of Tours"), [Peter](/wiki/Saint_Peter "Saint Peter"), and [Paul](/wiki/Paul_the_Apostle "Paul the Apostle").[[13]](#cite_note-Philippe_George-13) The [relics](/wiki/Relic "Relic") of Saint Remaclus were housed in this new church.[[14]](#cite_note-Blick_and_Tekippe-14)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/Principality_of_Stavelot-Malm%C3%A9dy.jpg/250px-Principality_of_Stavelot-Malm%C3%A9dy.jpg)](/wiki/File:Principality_of_Stavelot-Malm%C3%A9dy.jpg)

Territory of Stavelot-Malmedy

### Development and the High Middle Ages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Princely_Abbey_of_Stavelot-Malmedy&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Development and the High Middle Ages")]

[![Through the archway of a gate is visible a wide part of the red-brick façade of a two-storey building, with large windows outlines with white stone arches. They grey roof shows skylight windows of a third floor. The entrance is preceded by a small set of steps and is surmounted by an ornament showing two figures supporting a coat of arms.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/Stavelot_JPG02.jpg/250px-Stavelot_JPG02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stavelot_JPG02.jpg)

[Stavelot Abbey](/wiki/Stavelot_Abbey "Stavelot Abbey")

[![A courtyard is surrounded by a stone building, with ivy growing around wooden-framed windows.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/Malmedy_JPG02.jpg/250px-Malmedy_JPG02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Malmedy_JPG02.jpg)

[Malmedy Abbey](/wiki/Malmedy_Abbey "Malmedy Abbey")

In 747, [Carloman, Duke of the Franks and Mayor of the Palace of Austrasia](/wiki/Carloman_(mayor_of_the_palace) "Carloman (mayor of the palace)"), enlarged the abbeys' lands with gifts from his own, on his [abdication](/wiki/Abdication "Abdication").[[15]](#cite_note-Carolingians_p59-15) Throughout the ninth century, the abbeys played an important cultural role in [Lotharingia](/wiki/Lotharingia "Lotharingia"), particularly thanks to abbot [Christian](/wiki/Christian_of_Stavelot "Christian of Stavelot"). Around 875, the relics of [St Quirinus](/wiki/Nicasius,_Quirinus,_Scubiculus,_and_Pientia "Nicasius, Quirinus, Scubiculus, and Pientia") were [translated](/wiki/Translation_(relic) "Translation (relic)") from *[Gasny](/wiki/Gasny "Gasny")* to Malmedy Abbey after the intercession of Emperor [Charles the Bald](/wiki/Charles_the_Bald "Charles the Bald"), partly to secure relics comparable to those of St. Remaclus at Stavelot.[[16]](#cite_note-Roetgen-Rott:Quirinus-16)

Through the seventh and eighth centuries, the two abbeys followed their mission of [evangelism](/wiki/Evangelism "Evangelism"), along with [forest clearance](/wiki/Logging "Logging"). With the decline of the [Carolingian Empire](/wiki/Carolingian_Empire "Carolingian Empire"), however, the abbeys suffered the same decay as elsewhere, leaving the principality in the custody of [lay abbots](/wiki/Lay_abbot "Lay abbot")—temporal guardians—from 844 to 938, including [Ebbo](/wiki/Ebbo "Ebbo"), [Archbishop of Reims](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_Reims "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Reims"), [Adalard the Seneschal](/wiki/Adalard_the_Seneschal "Adalard the Seneschal"), and [Reginar](/wiki/Reginar,_Duke_of_Lorraine "Reginar, Duke of Lorraine") and [Giselbert](/wiki/Gilbert,_Duke_of_Lorraine "Gilbert, Duke of Lorraine"), dukes of Lorraine.[[17]](#cite_note-Étude-17)[[18]](#cite_note-Burkes:Rainier_Longneck-18)

Welcoming [pilgrims](/wiki/Pilgrimage "Pilgrimage") and the sick was a part of the monks' mission. The **Miracula sancti Remacli** mention the **[xenodochium](/wiki/Xenodochium "Xenodochium")**, the monastery's [hospice](/wiki/Hospice "Hospice"), where poor pilgrims were granted hospitality, including food for almost eight days, whilst they made their [devotions](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_devotions "Roman Catholic devotions"); this hospice differs from the abbey's [hospital](/wiki/Hospital "Hospital"): **hospitale coenobii**. On 13 April 862, [Lothair II of Lotharingia](/wiki/Lothair_II "Lothair II"), while dealing with the distribution of property **ad hospital ejusdem coenobii**, ordered that local [tithes](/wiki/Tithe "Tithe") be paid to the hospital **absque netligentia et tarditate**, an order he confirmed on 10 June 873.[[13]](#cite_note-Philippe_George-13)

In December 881, [Normans](/wiki/Normans "Normans"), including [Godfrid, Duke of Frisia](/wiki/Godfrid,_Duke_of_Frisia "Godfrid, Duke of Frisia"), invaded the area, burning both abbeys and causing the monks to flee with their treasures and relics.[[14]](#cite_note-Blick_and_Tekippe-14)[[17]](#cite_note-Étude-17)[[19]](#cite_note-Annals_of_Fulda-19)[[20]](#cite_note-Dewez-20)[[21]](#cite_note-Mélanges-21) Several historical sources provide evidence of the raid of 881, which was well prepared and organised. The monks rushed to dig up the relics of Remaclus and fled to the [county of *Porcien*](/w/index.php?title=County_of_Porcien&action=edit&redlink=1 "County of Porcien (page does not exist)") in present-day *[Bogny-sur-Meuse](/wiki/Bogny-sur-Meuse "Bogny-sur-Meuse")*, in the French *Ardennes*; the surrounding region was largely unaffected by the invasion.[[13]](#cite_note-Philippe_George-13) Stavelot and Malmedy were both burned, with the monks not returning until just before Christmas 882, with a stay in *[Chooz](/wiki/Chooz,_Ardennes "Chooz, Ardennes")*, to allow them to repair the roofs of the monastic buildings. Relics from [Aachen](/wiki/Aachen "Aachen"), which had been entrusted to the monks at Stavelot because of the Norman threat, were returned intact.[[13]](#cite_note-Philippe_George-13) In gratitude, on 13 November 882, [Charles the Fat](/wiki/Charles_the_Fat "Charles the Fat")—Carolingian emperor and king of East Francia, [Alemannia](/wiki/Duke_of_Swabia "Duke of Swabia"), and [Italy](/wiki/King_of_Italy "King of Italy")—granted the abbeys the lands of *Blendef*, a dependency of *[Louveigné](/wiki/Louveign%C3%A9 "Louveigné")*, and restored to them the chapel in *Bra* (now a part of *[Lierneux](/wiki/Lierneux "Lierneux")* in *[Liège](/wiki/Li%C3%A8ge_(province) "Liège (province)")*).[[13]](#cite_note-Philippe_George-13)

In 885, Normans extracted [ransom](/wiki/Ransom "Ransom") from *[Hesbaye](/wiki/Hesbaye "Hesbaye")* and passed through the Meuse valley, marching on *[Prüm](/wiki/Pr%C3%BCm "Prüm")*, causing the monks of Stavelot to flee again, finding refuge in the [county of *Logne*](/wiki/County_of_Logne "County of Logne") and *[Chèvremont](/wiki/Kerkrade "Kerkrade")*; the **Miracula Remacli** details the flight from the invaders and follows the monks' wanderings.[[13]](#cite_note-Philippe_George-13) After the invasions, abbot Odilon began to rebuild the ruined abbey of Stavelot, with support from [bishops of *Liège*](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Diocese_of_Li%C3%A8ge "Roman Catholic Diocese of Liège")—including [Notker](/wiki/Notker_of_Li%C3%A8ge "Notker of Liège"), the first prince-bishop. The abbots Odilon and Werinfride rebuilt the abbeys, with new building; re-established the monastic community; re-organised the principality. By the time of the [Ottonian dynasty](/wiki/Ottonian_dynasty "Ottonian dynasty") in the early 10th century, the abbeys were once again of suitable Imperial stature.[[17]](#cite_note-Étude-17) A new abbey church was built in Malmedy in 992, dedicated to St Quirinus;[[16]](#cite_note-Roetgen-Rott:Quirinus-16) in 1007, a parish church was consecrated to [Saint Gereon](/wiki/Gereon "Gereon").[[7]](#cite_note-fotw:Malmedy-7)

Another danger threatened the abbey—and the Western Empire—in the 10th century: the [Hungarian invasions](/wiki/Hungarian_invasions_of_Europe "Hungarian invasions of Europe"). Having been deposed as [duke of Lotharingia](/wiki/Duke_of_Lotharingia "Duke of Lotharingia"), [Conrad the Red](/wiki/Conrad,_Duke_of_Lorraine "Conrad, Duke of Lorraine") invited the Hungarians to undermine his opponents, [Bruno the Great](/wiki/Bruno_the_Great "Bruno the Great"), [archbishop of Cologne](/wiki/Archbishop_of_Cologne "Archbishop of Cologne"), and [Reginar III, Count of Hainaut](/wiki/Reginar_III,_Count_of_Hainaut "Reginar III, Count of Hainaut"). The **Annales Stabulensis** reports: **Anno 954 Ungri populantur regiones Galliæ ... Anno 955. Victoria de Ungris** ["In the year 954, Hungarians ravage the regions of Gaul ... In the year 955, victory over the Hungarians"].[[13]](#cite_note-Philippe_George-13) On 1 July 960, Eraclus, bishop of *Liège*, driven by the fears of the time, granted the monks a place to build a refuge in *Liège*, although five years earlier, the victory of emperor [Otto I](/wiki/Otto_I,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Otto I, Holy Roman Emperor") over the Hungarians at [Lechfeld](/wiki/Battle_of_Lechfeld_(955) "Battle of Lechfeld (955)") had removed the danger of Hungarian [sack](/wiki/Looting "Looting").[[13]](#cite_note-Philippe_George-13)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/Heinrich_III..jpg/250px-Heinrich_III..jpg)](/wiki/File:Heinrich_III..jpg)

[Henry III, Holy Roman Emperor](/wiki/Henry_III,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Henry III, Holy Roman Emperor"), who was present for the 1040 [consecration](/wiki/Consecration "Consecration") of the church built in Stavelot under prince-abbot [Poppo of Deinze](/wiki/Poppo_of_Stavelot "Poppo of Stavelot").

The key building period at the abbey of Stavelot corresponds to the rule of prince-abbot [Poppo of Deinze](/wiki/Poppo_of_Stavelot "Poppo of Stavelot"), the second founder of the abbey,[[22]](#cite_note-Abbaye_de_Stavelot-22) who was made abbot by [Holy Roman Emperor](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Emperor "Holy Roman Emperor") [Henry II](/wiki/Henry_II,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Henry II, Holy Roman Emperor") in 1020.[[23]](#cite_note-cath:St._Poppo-23) He built an imposing church over 100 metres (330 ft) in length, which was consecrated in the presence of [Emperor Henry III](/wiki/Henry_III,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Henry III, Holy Roman Emperor") on 5 June 1040.[[13]](#cite_note-Philippe_George-13) Thietmar was the lay patron who assembled carpenters and stonemasons to build the abbey church.[[24]](#cite_note-Vita_Popponis-24) As well as confirming the authenticity of the relics of St Quirinus at Malmedy in 1042,[[16]](#cite_note-Roetgen-Rott:Quirinus-16) Poppo revived the cult of St Remaclus. Poppo died in 1048; his cult, which began almost immediately,[[24]](#cite_note-Vita_Popponis-24) focused on his resting place in the crypt. Malmedy developed around the monastery; until the end of the tenth century, the villagers used the chapel of [Saint Laurent](/wiki/Lawrence_of_Rome "Lawrence of Rome"), an [apsidiole](/wiki/Apsidiole "Apsidiole") of the abbey church, as their place of worship.[[7]](#cite_note-fotw:Malmedy-7)

In 1065, controversy arose when [Anno II, Archbishop of Cologne](/wiki/Anno_II "Anno II"), named Tegernon of *Brauweiler* abbot of Malmedy, on dubious authority—whilst Malmedy was in the archdiocese of Cologne, the two abbeys were linked and, thus, under the purview of the diocese of *Liège*, where Stavelot lay. This occurred despite several previous Imperial bulls reinforcing the position that the two abbeys should be subject to a single abbot.[[25]](#cite_note-Laws:p8,_10-25) The monks from Stavelot processed to Malmedy with the [crosier](/wiki/Crosier "Crosier") and relics of St Remaclus to remind the rebellious monks of the traditional ordering of the abbeys that the saint had instituted. The relics and crosier were also transported to an Imperial Diet of [Henry IV](/wiki/Henry_IV,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Henry IV, Holy Roman Emperor") in [Goslar](/wiki/Goslar "Goslar"). In 1066, they processed again, this time to [Aachen](/wiki/Aachen "Aachen") and [Fritzlar](/wiki/Fritzlar "Fritzlar"); they processed to [Bitburg](/wiki/Bitburg "Bitburg") and [Bamberg](/wiki/Bamberg "Bamberg") the following year. That their prayers were not answered apparently led the monks to despair that the relics were becoming impotent or that the monks were being punished by their patron; in 1067 and 1068, abbot Thierry even went to [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome") to appeal to [Pope Alexander II](/wiki/Pope_Alexander_II "Pope Alexander II"). This impasse lasted for a further three years, until Henry held court at Liège during Easter in 1071; with great ceremony, the monks processed with Remaclus's relics to meet with his legendary fellow bishop [St Lambert](/wiki/Lambert_of_Maastricht "Lambert of Maastricht"), joined en route by the relics of [St Symmetrus](/w/index.php?title=St_Symmetrus&action=edit&redlink=1 "St Symmetrus (page does not exist)"). Numerous miracles convinced the emperor to recognise the union of the two abbeys and reiterate the superiority of Stavelot, forcing Anno eventually to capitulate. A rejoiceful procession back to Stavelot paused en route to celebrate Mass on the banks of the Meuse; finally, the monks processed with Remaclus's relics to the abbey at Malmedy, to symbolise the restoration of his and their authority. This series of episodes is recounted in the heroic narrative of the *Triumph of St Remaclus* and confirmed by several contemporary sources.[[14]](#cite_note-Blick_and_Tekippe-14)

In 1098, [Wibald](/wiki/Wibald "Wibald") was born in the hamlet of *Chevrouheid*, near Stavelot. Elected prince-abbot in 1130, he played a key role in the religious life of the region and the abbeys. In 1138, he granted permission for the [castle](/wiki/Castle_of_Logne "Castle of Logne") to be built in *Logne*, first mentioned in an 862 abbey charter.[[26]](#cite_note-Laws:p12:1138-06-05-26)[[27]](#cite_note-Patrimoine:Huy-27) In the 12th to 15th centuries, however, the abbacy experienced a slow decline. In the 14th and 15th centuries, several Imperial edicts, initially issued by [Emperor Charles IV](/wiki/Charles_IV,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Charles IV, Holy Roman Emperor"), put the abbacy under the protection of the [counts of Luxembourg](/wiki/County_of_Luxembourg "County of Luxembourg").[[28]](#cite_note-Laws:p14-5-28)

### Early Modern Age

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Princely_Abbey_of_Stavelot-Malmedy&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Early Modern Age")]

In 1509, [William](/w/index.php?title=William_of_Manderscheid&action=edit&redlink=1 "William of Manderscheid (page does not exist)") of *[Manderscheid](/wiki/County_of_Manderscheid "County of Manderscheid")* organised a procession to induce the recalcitrant county of *Logne*, a fief of the abbey, to submit to his jurisdiction. The cortège was pious, rather than fraught with tension; with Stavelot monks carrying the shrines of Remaclus and *Babolene* with other reliquaries; and the monks of Malmedy with reliquaries of [Quirinus](/wiki/Nicasius,_Quirinus,_Scubiculus,_and_Pientia "Nicasius, Quirinus, Scubiculus, and Pientia"), [Just](/wiki/Justus_of_Beauvais "Justus of Beauvais"), Peter, and Philip; joined by parishioners from *Lierneux* with the relics of Symmetrus.[[14]](#cite_note-Blick_and_Tekippe-14) In 1521, after the castle in *Logne* had been dismantled, William added "Count of *Logne*" to the abbots' titles, with the county representing most of the western portion of the principality's territory.[[27]](#cite_note-Patrimoine:Huy-27)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/Stavelot_et_Abbaye.JPG/250px-Stavelot_et_Abbaye.JPG)](/wiki/File:Stavelot_et_Abbaye.JPG)

The town and abbey of Stavelot, c. 1735

The abbey church served as a monastic church and as a church of pilgrimage until the French Revolution. Its imposing gatehouse tower was rebuilt in 1534;[[22]](#cite_note-Abbaye_de_Stavelot-22) (its ground floor and some further foundation still remain). Malmedy began to flourish particularly in the 16th century with the development of tannery; in 1544 there were only 216 houses with a thousand inhabitants, but that more than tripled by 1635.[[7]](#cite_note-fotw:Malmedy-7)

After the death of abbot Christopher of Manderscheid, there was a series of absent abbots, including [Maximilian Henry of Bavaria](/wiki/Maximilian_Henry_of_Bavaria "Maximilian Henry of Bavaria") (also bishop of *Liège* and of *[Hildesheim](/wiki/Bishopric_of_Hildesheim "Bishopric of Hildesheim")*), who reformed the abbey in 1656.[[10]](#cite_note-Délices-10) In the 17th century, Stavelot and Malmedy were major centres of [tanning](/wiki/Tanning_(leather) "Tanning (leather)") in Europe.[[9]](#cite_note-Liège:Stavelot-9)[[11]](#cite_note-1911:Malmedy-11)[[12]](#cite_note-Malmedy:Growth-12) [Papermaking](/wiki/Papermaking "Papermaking") was particularly important to Malmedy, as was the manufacture of [gunpowder](/wiki/Gunpowder "Gunpowder"). Other industries included [cotton](/wiki/Cotton "Cotton") manufacturing, manufacture of [chess sets](/wiki/Chess_set "Chess set") and [dominoes](/wiki/Dominoes "Dominoes"), and [gingerbread](/wiki/Gingerbread "Gingerbread") baking.[[11]](#cite_note-1911:Malmedy-11)[[12]](#cite_note-Malmedy:Growth-12) In 1659, a [Capuchin](/wiki/Order_of_Friars_Minor_Capuchin "Order of Friars Minor Capuchin") convent was built in Stavelot.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/Stavelot_Delmotte_1760.jpg/250px-Stavelot_Delmotte_1760.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stavelot_Delmotte_1760.jpg)

Prince-abbot *Alexandre Delmotte* (1753–1766)

Despite the abbacy's [neutrality](/wiki/Neutral_country "Neutral country") and the protection of the prince-abbots, the territory was invaded at least 50 times by troops passing through, whose depredations had disastrous consequences for the population,[[12]](#cite_note-Malmedy:Growth-12) including the 4 October 1689 razing of both Stavelot[[29]](#cite_note-Kellen-29) and Malmedy[[7]](#cite_note-fotw:Malmedy-7)[[12]](#cite_note-Malmedy:Growth-12)[[29]](#cite_note-Kellen-29) on the orders of *[Nicolas Catinat](/wiki/Nicolas_Catinat "Nicolas Catinat")*, general to [Louis XIV of France](/wiki/Louis_XIV_of_France "Louis XIV of France"), during the [Nine Years' War](/wiki/Nine_Years%27_War "Nine Years' War").[[7]](#cite_note-fotw:Malmedy-7)[[12]](#cite_note-Malmedy:Growth-12) In Stavelot, the entire town, including over 360 houses, was destroyed, leaving just the abbey and its farmyard standing.[[30]](#cite_note-Études_historiques-30) In Malmedy, some 600 out of the 660 houses of the town were destroyed and it took more than a century to completely rebuild.[[7]](#cite_note-fotw:Malmedy-7) Malmedy's 1601 [city walls](/wiki/Defensive_wall "Defensive wall") had previously been destroyed by French troops in 1658, during the 1635–59 [Franco-Spanish War](/wiki/Franco-Spanish_War_(1635%E2%80%9359) "Franco-Spanish War (1635–59)").[[7]](#cite_note-fotw:Malmedy-7) The wars—and passage by troops of [Brandenburg-Prussia](/wiki/Brandenburg-Prussia "Brandenburg-Prussia"), the [Dutch Republic](/wiki/Dutch_Republic "Dutch Republic"), [France](/wiki/Early_modern_France "Early modern France"), and *Liège*—had cost the principality the sum of 2.75 million **[Reichsthaler](/wiki/Reichsthaler "Reichsthaler")**. The abbey had to borrow 134 000 thalers from Liège and [Verviers](/wiki/Verviers "Verviers"); another loan, shared amongst the communities, totalled 109 000 thalers, with annual [interest](/wiki/Interest "Interest") of 14 161 thalers and [arrears](/wiki/Arrears "Arrears") of 26 000 thalers.[[30]](#cite_note-Études_historiques-30)

By the start of the 18th century the principality had lost a third of its territory, as a result of war, fires, pillage, and unjust encroachments. The deputies to the Imperial Diet complained that, in the 16th century, the [Spanish Netherlands](/wiki/Spanish_Netherlands "Spanish Netherlands") had seized several territories and that the Bishopric of *Liège* had stolen over half a dozen **seigneuries** totalling over 2000 households; adding that the principality itself retained only 1693 households, having had 3780 households before the upheavals and that the suffering of the principality had caused some of the richest and most powerful families to emigrate. The Imperial Diet was moved to halve the **[Reichsmatrikel](/wiki/Imperial_Register "Imperial Register")** for the abbeys (reducing the sums and troops the abbeys needed to provide towards the Imperial army) and exempting any need for the abbeys to send troops to the Imperial army for three years, an exemption extended for four more years on 24 March 1715.[[30]](#cite_note-Études_historiques-30)

### Abolition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Princely_Abbey_of_Stavelot-Malmedy&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Abolition")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/Stavelot_Thys.jpg/250px-Stavelot_Thys.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stavelot_Thys.jpg)

*Célestin Thys*, the last prince-abbot

During the [French Revolutionary Wars](/wiki/French_Revolutionary_Wars "French Revolutionary Wars"), from 1793 to 1804, the abbey was abandoned by the monks and the principality extinguished.[[31]](#cite_note-fotw:Stavelot-31) Stavelot was incorporated into the French Republic by a decree of 2 March 1793, along with *[Franchimont](/wiki/Franchimont_Castle "Franchimont Castle")* and *Logne*.[[32]](#cite_note-Duvergier-32) Despite opposition from local notables, Malmedy was similarly incorporated by a decree of *9 [Vendémiaire](/wiki/Vend%C3%A9miaire "Vendémiaire")* of the Year IV (1 October 1795).[[7]](#cite_note-fotw:Malmedy-7) Stavelot abbey itself was sacked and the church sold and demolished;[[31]](#cite_note-fotw:Stavelot-31)[[33]](#cite_note-Exposé_fidèle-33) of the church just the western doorway remains, as a free-standing tower. Two [cloisters](/wiki/Cloister "Cloister")—one secular, one for the monks—survive as the courtyards of the brick-and-stone 17th-century domestic ranges. The foundations of the abbey church are presented as a footprint, with walls and column bases that enable the visitor to visualize the scale of the [Romanesque](/wiki/Romanesque_architecture "Romanesque architecture") abbey.[[22]](#cite_note-Abbaye_de_Stavelot-22)

## Geography and administration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Princely_Abbey_of_Stavelot-Malmedy&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Geography and administration")]

[![A small stone church stands in a field. The apse shows two small stained-glass windows, with empty arched niches above. The left transept is also visible, with a half-height chapel adjacent.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/Xhignesse_JPG02.jpg/250px-Xhignesse_JPG02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Xhignesse_JPG02.jpg)

11th-century church of *[St Médard](/wiki/Medardus "Medardus")* in *[Xhignesse](/wiki/Xhignesse "Xhignesse")*

Based largely in the *[Amblève](/wiki/Ambl%C3%A8ve_(river) "Amblève (river)")* and *[Ourthe](/wiki/Ourthe "Ourthe")* river valleys, the principality occupied a substantial proportion of what is now the [*arrondissement* of *Verviers*](/wiki/Arrondissement_of_Verviers "Arrondissement of Verviers") in the [province of *Liège*](/wiki/Li%C3%A8ge_(province) "Liège (province)"). By the time of the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution"), the principality was bounded on the north by the [duchy of Limburg](/wiki/Duchy_of_Limburg "Duchy of Limburg"), on the south and east by the [duchy of Luxembourg](/wiki/Duchy_of_Luxembourg "Duchy of Luxembourg") and on the north-west by the [marquisate of *Franchimont*](/wiki/Marquisate_of_Franchimont "Marquisate of Franchimont") and the *[Condroz](/wiki/Condroz "Condroz")*.[[30]](#cite_note-Études_historiques-30) The principality was divided into three administrative districts: the **postelleries** of Stavelot and Malmedy, and the [county of *Logne*](/wiki/County_of_Logne "County of Logne"), totalling around 28,000 inhabitants.[[6]](#cite_note-em:Stavelot-6)[[12]](#cite_note-Malmedy:Growth-12)[[30]](#cite_note-Études_historiques-30)[[34]](#cite_note-Büsching-34) The **postellerie** of Stavelot contained 14 communities and that of Malmedy contained the town itself and the **bans** of *[Waimes](/wiki/Waimes "Waimes")* and *[Francorchamps](/wiki/Francorchamps "Francorchamps")*. The county of *Logne* was divided into four **quartiers**: *[Hamoir](/wiki/Hamoir "Hamoir")* (7 communities), *[Ocquier](/wiki/Ocquier "Ocquier")* (6), *[Comblain](/wiki/Comblain-Fairon "Comblain-Fairon")* (5) and *[Louveigné](/wiki/Louveign%C3%A9 "Louveigné")* (2), with public assemblies being based in *[Bernardfagne](/wiki/Bernardfagne "Bernardfagne")*. In addition, six other communities were [exclaves](/wiki/Enclave_and_exclave "Enclave and exclave"), and there were the **seigneuries** of [Anthisnes](/wiki/Anthisnes "Anthisnes") and *Vien*, in the *Confroz*. In 1768, these two **seigneuries** were exchanged with *Liège* for *[Chooz](/wiki/Chooz,_Ardennes "Chooz, Ardennes")*, *Sclessin*, and *Ougrée*.[[30]](#cite_note-Études_historiques-30)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Stavelot_Lorc%C3%A9_c.1905.jpg/250px-Stavelot_Lorc%C3%A9_c.1905.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stavelot_Lorc%C3%A9_c.1905.jpg)

*Lorcé* belonged to Stavelot, like a number of other villages in the *Ardennes*

Several sources note that there were disputes between the two abbeys, with Stavelot assuming primacy over Malmedy,[[35]](#cite_note-Laws:p12:1137-09-22-35) to the latter's discontent; though new abbots were invested in Stavelot on behalf of both abbeys.[[34]](#cite_note-Büsching-34)[[36]](#cite_note-em:Malmedy-36) Whilst an [absolute](/wiki/Absolute_monarchy "Absolute monarchy") principality, in some matters the prince-abbot would consult a general assembly or **états** of clergy, dignitaries, prince's officers, mayors, and aldermen, whose main role was to vote for [taxes](/wiki/Tax "Tax"). Each of the three districts had its own provincial assembly and court, with a Princely Council for highly contested cases. As a court of last resort, citizens could appeal to the **[Reichskammergericht](/wiki/Reichskammergericht "Reichskammergericht")** (Imperial Chamber Court), created by [Emperor Maximilian I](/wiki/Maximilian_I,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Maximilian I, Holy Roman Emperor") (reigned 1508–19) on the model of the parliaments in [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris") and [Mechelen](/wiki/Mechelen "Mechelen") and headquartered in [Frankfurt](/wiki/Frankfurt "Frankfurt") (1495–1527), *[Speyer](/wiki/Speyer "Speyer")* (1527–1693) and [Wetzlar](/wiki/Wetzlar "Wetzlar") (1693–1806).[[1]](#cite_note-Uyttebrouck-1)

Shortly before the principality's extinction, it contributed just over 81 Reichsthaler per session for the maintenance of the Imperial Chamber Court, from annual revenues of around 25 000 [Rhenish guilder](/wiki/Rhenish_guilder "Rhenish guilder").[[6]](#cite_note-em:Stavelot-6)[[34]](#cite_note-Büsching-34)[[36]](#cite_note-em:Malmedy-36)

## Art

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Princely_Abbey_of_Stavelot-Malmedy&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Art")]

The abbeys at Stavelot and Malmedy commissioned some of the finest surviving works of [Mosan art](/wiki/Mosan_art "Mosan art"), one of the leading schools of [Romanesque art](/wiki/Romanesque_art "Romanesque art"), especially in [goldsmith](/wiki/Goldsmith "Goldsmith") metalwork, which was then the most prestigious art form. Their collections were dispersed by wars and, finally, the French Revolution. Works from the abbeys are now in museums across the world. The [illuminated manuscript](/wiki/Illuminated_manuscript "Illuminated manuscript") [Stavelot Bible](/wiki/Stavelot_Bible "Stavelot Bible") (now in the [British Library](/wiki/British_Library "British Library")) was probably the abbey's main bible, and was created there by several hands over a four-year period ending in 1097[[37]](#cite_note-bl:Stavelot_Bible-37) (other works have been identified as being from the same [scriptorium](/wiki/Scriptorium "Scriptorium")). The bible has been described as "a perfect microcosm of the influences and interests that gave rise to the first [Romanesque painting](/wiki/Romanesque_art#Painting "Romanesque art")".[[38]](#cite_note-eb:Western_painting-38) A group of manuscripts from the less productive [scriptorium](/wiki/Scriptorium "Scriptorium") at Malmedy were donated to the [Vatican Library](/wiki/Vatican_Library "Vatican Library") in 1816 by [Pope Pius VII](/wiki/Pope_Pius_VII "Pope Pius VII"),[[39]](#cite_note-Paul_Saenger-39) including the *Malmedy Bible* and two [lectionaries](/wiki/Lectionary "Lectionary") from about 1300.[[40]](#cite_note-Judith_Oliver-40) Malmedy illuminations show a particular closeness with metalwork styles.[[41]](#cite_note-Lapière-41)

Abbot [Wibald](/wiki/Wibald "Wibald") (ruled 1130–58) was an important Imperial minister and diplomat, and was regarded as one of the greatest patrons of Mosan art in its best period, although much of the evidence for this is circumstantial. Some of his surviving letters discuss works which may be identifiable with existing pieces, and an "aurifaber G", who some have identified with *[Godefroid de Claire](/wiki/Godefroid_de_Claire "Godefroid de Claire")*, a shadowy figure to whom many masterpieces are attributed. Several important commissions were certainly placed by Wibald with Mosan workshops of goldsmiths and metalworkers, and other works later connected with Stavelot are also presumed to have been commissioned by him.[[42]](#cite_note-grove:Wibald-42) The works, mostly *[champlevé](/wiki/Champlev%C3%A9 "Champlevé")* [enamels](/wiki/Vitreous_enamel "Vitreous enamel") of very high quality, include the [Stavelot Triptych](/wiki/Stavelot_Triptych "Stavelot Triptych"), a portable altar [reliquary](/wiki/Reliquary "Reliquary") for two fragments of the [True Cross](/wiki/True_Cross "True Cross"), c. 1156, (now in the [Morgan Library & Museum](/wiki/Morgan_Library_%26_Museum "Morgan Library & Museum") in [New York](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City")),[[31]](#cite_note-fotw:Stavelot-31)[[43]](#cite_note-Corsair:Stavelot_Reliquary-43)[[44]](#cite_note-augusta:Stavelot_Triptych-44) the Stavelot Portable Altar of 1146, and a head-shaped reliquary of [Pope Alexander II](/wiki/Pope_Alexander_II "Pope Alexander II"), c. 1150, possibly by *Godefroid* (both now [*Cinquantenaire* Museum](/wiki/Cinquantenaire_Museum "Cinquantenaire Museum"), [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels")).[[45]](#cite_note-Hanns_Swarzenski-45) A gold [relief](/wiki/Relief "Relief") [retable](/wiki/Retable "Retable") of the [Pentecost](/wiki/Pentecost "Pentecost") (1160–70) is in the **[Musée national du Moyen Âge](/wiki/Mus%C3%A9e_national_du_Moyen_%C3%82ge "Musée national du Moyen Âge")** in Paris. An important and more elaborate retable of Saint Remaclus, of about 1150, about nine square metres in extent, was broken up during the French Revolution; and only two round enamel plaques survive, in Berlin and [Frankfurt](/wiki/Frankfurt "Frankfurt"),[[46]](#cite_note-met:Low_Countries-46) though a 17th-century drawing survives in *Liège*.[[42]](#cite_note-grove:Wibald-42)[[47]](#cite_note-Godefridus_Snoek-47)

## Coat of arms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Princely_Abbey_of_Stavelot-Malmedy&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Coat of arms")]

The [coat of arms](/wiki/Coat_of_arms "Coat of arms") granted to the town of Stavelot, in 1819, is also that of the abbey—parted [fesswise](/wiki/Fess "Fess") between an image of St Remaclus and the [wolf](/wiki/Gray_wolf "Gray wolf"), which in Stavelot's founding legend carried bricks for the building of the abbey after having killed Remaclus's [donkey](/wiki/Donkey "Donkey").[[10]](#cite_note-Délices-10)[[31]](#cite_note-fotw:Stavelot-31)[[48]](#cite_note-hotw:Stavelot-48)
